content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission,
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

September 2, 1976

Mr. Spencer E. Covert, Jr.
Alexander Bowie Law Corporation
610 Newport Center Drive - Suite 1220
Newport Beach, California 92660

Dear Mr. Covert:

Thank you for your letter of August 30, 1976, regarding
the repeal of Regulation §18700(a)(1)(C). Your letter will
be forwarded to the Commission for consideration at its meet-
ing of September 7 and 8, 1976.

Sincerely,

Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr., Chief
Conflicts of Interest Division

DLS:gb

CALIFORNIA (SEAL) 322 6641 • ENFORCEMENT-CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322 6441

ALEXANDER BOWIE
A LAW CORPORATION

610 NEWPORT CENTER DRIVE · SUITE 1220
NEWPORT BEACH, CALIFORNIA 92660

ALEXANDER BOWIE                                                    AREA CODE 714
CLAYTON H. PARKER          SPENCER E. COVERT, JR.               TELEPHONE 644-9311
NANCY C. SHANAHAN          PATRICIA REILLY                      REF. OUR FILE

August 30, 1976


Fair Political Practices Commission
Attention: Daniel Lowenstein, Chairman
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA  95814

Re: Meeting of September 7, 8, 1976
    Consideration of repeal of Regulation
    18700 (a)(1)(C)

Dear Commissioners:

     It is our understanding that the Fair Political
Practices Commission (Commission) will consider repealing
the referenced regulation during its September meeting.
This letter is written on behalf of the Irvine Unified
School District, Newport-Mesa Unified School District, Ocean
View School District, and Orange Unified School District,
all of which are located in Orange County, California. Each
of these school districts supports repeal of Section 18700
(a)(1)(C).

     The reason for our concern relates to parent
advisory committees for federally funded projects and whether
persons serving on these committees must be designated for
disclosure purposes under local Conflict of Interest Codes.
At the present time Section 18700 (a)(1)(C) includes these
persons within the definition of public official. The
reason why persons serving on parent advisory committees are
included is because their consideration is a legal prerequisite
to receiving federal funds.

     Each of these school districts is a recipient of
federal funds. Federal funds are used for programs such as
early childhood education and bilingual-bicultural programs.

ALEXANDER BOWIE
A LAW CORPORATION

Fair Political Practices Commission
August 30, 1976
Page 2


One of the prerequisites for receiving federal
funding for funding of certain programs is a parent advisory
committee. The parent advisory committee must review the
school district's program prior to the time that it is
submitted to the federal government for purposes of being
considered for federal funding. Each school district must
specifically state that a parent advisory committee has
considered the program that is being submitted to the federal
government. Should a school district's particular program
be approved, then the parent advisory committee will
advise the district regarding its implementation and effect
upon the community.

But for Section 18700 (a)(1)(C), these parent
advisory committees are not public officials within the
meaning of Section 18700 (a). They do not make final
governmental decisions regarding the submittal or implementation
of federally funded programs. These decisions are made by the
school board. A parent advisory committee does not compel a
school board to submit a program. The role of the parent
advisory group for federally funded programs is restricted
to consultation and recommendation. Its decisions are subject
to significant amendment or modification by the school
district.

A practical reason for objecting to Section 18700
(a)(1)(C) is that it will discourage persons to serve on
parent advisory groups. We are certain that the Commission has
often heard this objection raised about various provisions of
the Political Reform Act of 1974. Nevertheless, persons are
selected for service on parent advisory groups because these
persons come from the community that will be benefited by
the federally funded programs. These persons serve on a
voluntary basis. Quite frankly, the school districts are
concerned that disclosure requirements will unduly burden
and discourage persons from serving on parent advisory
groups. Nevertheless, establishment and use of such parent
advisory groups are legal prerequisites for receiving federal
funds under the Elementary and Secondary Education Act and
similar federally funded programs.

ALEXANDER BOWIE
A LAW CORPORATION

Fair Political Practices Commission
August 30, 1976
Page 3

For the above stated reasons, we believe that
Section 18700 (a)(1)(C) should be repealed. Your consideration
of this request is deeply appreciated.

Very truly yours,

ALEXANDER BOWIE
A Law Corporation


By [illegible]
Spencer E. Covert, Jr.

Ocean View School District
Orange Unified School District
Newport-Mesa Unified School District

sections.question_synthetic: Should the Fair Political Practices Commission repeal Regulation 18700(a)(1)(c), which currently requires persons serving on parent advisory committees for federally funded projects to be designated as public officials for disclosure purposes under local conflict of interest codes?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter, not an FPPC response/advice letter. The document lacks typical Q/C sections. A separate cover letter from FPPC dated September 2, 1976 indicates the request will be forwarded to the Commission. The sender argues parent advisory committee members should not be classified as public officials since they only provide consultation and recommendations, with final decisions remaining with the school board.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Should the Fair Political Practices Commission repeal Regulation 18700(a)(1)(c), which currently requires persons serving on parent advisory committees for federally funded projects to be designated as public officials for disclosure purposes under local conflict of interest codes?

embedding.summary: This document contains a letter from Alexander Bowie, A Law Corporation, dated August 30, 1976, on behalf of four Orange County school districts requesting that the FPPC repeal Section 18700(a)(1)(c) because it classifies parent advisory committee members as public officials, which they argue discourages voluntary participation despite these committees being legally required for federal funding.
